About
Chris Morley has called the Village home since 2004, but his roots stretch from the outer boroughs to the Catskills, where he grew up around small businesses that shaped his entrepreneurial drive.
After studying history, psychology, and sociology at SUNY Binghamton — including a field study in West Africa — Chris began his career in finance. But the corporate world wasn’t for him. He soon discovered real estate, instantly fell in love with it, and quickly became an award-winning top producer.
Chris launched his real estate career in 2002, when the city was much quieter. At the time, he was told to work with clients “until they find a place, drop dead, or move to Jersey.” After realizing he was spending nearly half his day on the subway crisscrossing the city, Chris decided to focus on Greenwich Village and Chelsea. That decision paid off: he built lasting relationships with landlords, management companies, attorneys, mortgage brokers, and other brokers. Over the years, Chris has guided clients through every stage of life — from renting their first apartment to buying their first home, and now even helping their children do the same.
With more than two decades of experience, Chris has navigated multiple market cycles, using his education and instincts to predict trends and identify opportunities before they appear. His long-term property management experience gives him a unique edge: he knows exactly which upgrades are worth making — and which ones are not.
Chris is also on the preferred list of corporate relocation brokers for Google and several other Fortune 500 companies, trusted to help employees and executives transition smoothly into new homes in New York City.
In 2011, Chris returned to his small-business roots and founded Bien Realty. Tired of the rigidity of big firms, he wanted to create a boutique brokerage that could deliver the same level of service and resources as the largest companies, but with the determination, focus, and appreciation for relationships that only a small business can provide. Today, Bien Realty stands as one of the few independent brokerages still thriving in Greenwich Village — proof of Chris’s vision, persistence, and commitment to his clients.Show less

Downtown Manhattan real estate is not moving through a quiet moment. It is moving through a selective one. There is still significant money in the system, but it is not flowing everywhere equally. Luxury buyers remain active. Large deals still happen quietly. Capital is still showing up for trophy spending, prime product, and neighborhoods that continue to offer a strong mix of lifestyle, identity, and long-term relevance. At the same time, it is more expensive to operate and build real estate. Running a building costs more than many people realize. Development costs remain high. Materials matter. Financing matters. Holding the wrong asset matters even more. That is why this market can look confusing if you try to flatten it into one headline. Some people see uncertainty and conclude that everything is slowing down. I think the stronger interpretation is that capital is concentrating. The best neighborhoods, strongest buildings, and most resilient businesses are still attracting attention. The weaker ones are being tested in real time. That is true in real estate and in the broader economy. Capital is flowing aggressively into AI, but not every company exposed to that trend will make it through a rougher stretch. Every cycle rewards some businesses and exposes others. The same thing happens block by block in New York. The city keeps evolving in plain sight. One small but perfect example: a downtown gym with old boxing roots now hosting Muay Thai. The format changes. The energy stays. That is a good metaphor for Manhattan itself. It is one reason I stay focused on downtown Manhattan. Chelsea, Greenwich Village, West Village, SoHo, Flatiron, Tribeca, and FiDi are not just neighborhoods where people rent or buy homes. They are neighborhoods where capital, culture, reinvention, and daily life keep colliding. The strongest signal in this market is not the noise. It is where money is still willing to go. Downtown Manhattan real estate is being shaped by affordability pressure, selective confidence, supply constraints, and global capital flows, all at the same time. Mortgage rates are at their highest level since October, and that matters because it changes behavior quickly. The market is not frozen, but it is more payment-sensitive and more selective. Buyers are still moving, especially in Manhattan, but they are thinking harder about value, trade-offs, timing, and long-term fit. For first-time buyers and serious buyers alike, monthly payment math is carrying more weight. At the same time, Wall Street bonuses jumped again. The average bonus reached $246,900, and the total bonus pool hit a record $49.2 billion. Bloomberg also noted that bankers entered 2026 after record trading revenue and improved dealmaking. In downtown Manhattan neighborhoods like West Village, Chelsea, Greenwich Village, and SoHo, that still matters. Higher compensation at the top of the market continues to support confidence, liquidity, and willingness to transact. Housing supply remains one of the central forces underneath everything. Efforts to make it easier to build homes matter because supply constraints affect rents, pricing, and how quickly quality apartments move. You can see that pressure reflected not only in federal policy discussions, but also in local moves to fast-track affordable housing, support ADUs, and approve new rental development. At the same time, not every signal is clean. Some employers are still restructuring. Some office assets remain distressed. Consumer pricing in travel and entertainment is reminding everyone that affordability pressure is not limited to housing. But capital is still showing up where conviction is strongest. Refinancings are happening. Office-retail buildings are trading. Lenders are still backing major Manhattan towers. Large institutional players are still allocating capital globally. That is why the market feels so mixed. It is not dead. It is not simple. It is active, but more selective. Strong neighborhoods, strong product, and strong positioning still matter. For downtown Manhattan, that means the conversation is less about blanket market headlines and more about who is moving, why they are moving, what they can afford, and what kind of life they are trying to build around the move. People are still moving. They are just thinking harder. Bien Realty #NYCRealEstate #ManhattanRealEstate #DowntownManhattan #HousingMarket #BienRealty

There’s a common assumption that quantum computing is mainly about additional computational power. A faster engine. A more advanced processor. And while hardware progress absolutely matters, that framing misses what I think is more interesting. Quantum methodologies change how we represent problems. They offer different ways of structuring and navigating complex, high-dimensional data spaces. That shift in representation can matter just as much as raw speed. When you look across the pharmaceutical pipeline, nearly every AI application depends on extracting structure from messy, multi-modal biological data. Whether the goal is identifying the right patient cohort, refining clinical trial design, selecting biomarker panels, or interpreting imaging data, the model is only as good as the signal feeding it. If the signal is noisy, flattened, or poorly encoded, more classical compute does not automatically solve the problem. Over time, what we began exploring internally was the idea of quantum signal intelligence. Quantum systems are naturally suited to operate in high-dimensional spaces. They are built to encode complex correlations and preserve relationships that are difficult to capture with traditional representations. That does not mean we need fully mature quantum hardware to benefit from those ideas. Quantum-inspired methods allow us to apply principles from quantum mechanics to classical systems today. These approaches can run on existing hardware while introducing new ways of representing and navigating data. As quantum hardware matures, those same models have the potential to scale alongside it. This is less about replacing existing workflows and more about reframing how problems are structured. We are not biotech domain experts. We are quantum algorithm designers. That distinction is important. We are not here to dictate clinical strategy or biological interpretation. What we are interested in is identifying where signal degradation is materially constraining AI performance. Where are teams increasing compute but seeing marginal gains? Where do correlations likely exist in the data but remain inaccessible? Where is complexity being compressed in ways that flatten important structure? The pharmaceutical pipeline is filled with AI models. Improving the intelligence of the signal that feeds them may be one of the most practical ways to unlock meaningful performance gains. If that resonates, let’s explore it together. Check out our latest research: ingenii.io/tsi

Over the last several years, We've had the opportunity to work across imaging, drug discovery and clinical trials. At first glance, these fields feel distinct. But the deeper we went, the more a single theme emerged. Everything comes down to signal. Biological data is inherently complex. Interactions are layered, contextual, and often subtle. Classical methods do an incredible amount of heavy lifting, but as systems scale in dimensionality and interdependence, preserving meaningful structure becomes harder. That realization led to TSI: Translational Signal Intelligence. TSI reflects a shift in focus: instead of asking how to build better models on top of noisy foundations, we asked how to strengthen the foundation itself. Exploring quantum-inspired approaches gave us a different mathematical lens for representing complex correlations without flattening nuance. We’re encouraged by the early results. But more than that, we’re committed to more deeply understanding where this translates into real value in the pharmaceutical pipeline, where clearer signals lead to better decisions. Because across domains, modalities, and use cases...signal is the common denominator. If this resonates, get in touch! https://www.ingenii.io/tsi Laia Domingo, PhD

Thumbnail image
It all started when I pulled up to the event. Alone. Unbothered. You feel a room before you even step in. Heads turn. Eyes glance. Even the ones who look like me. I almost respect it. Because it shows people are paying attention. But it also shows something else. People still try to size you up before you speak. I grew up hearing a song: “Before I’d be a slave, I’d be buried in my grave.” I sang it as a kid. I did not know what it meant. I do now. I was raised in a house with a mix of cultures. Syrian. Indian. Lebanese. My stepfather is Black and Chinese. We had a woman who lived with us. Her name was Vie. She: • Cooked • Cleaned • Got me ready for school • Made sure I did what I had to do She raised me in a lot of ways. I remember hearing her sing that song while doing my hair. Or while she was cooking. At the time, it meant nothing to me. Now I understand. That song came from something real. From people who lived through it. Black Americans lived that. And one day it hit me. That is why Vie sang it. She would never accept being less. That line was clear. That stayed with me. I grew up in Fairfield County. People assume that means I do not know what it feels like to be treated as less. That is not true. I have walked into rooms and felt it right away. You see it in how people move. My experience as a woman has not been the same in every room. It does not matter what you know. It does not matter what you built. I have been in rooms where I felt underestimated before I spoke. I have had someone say they were “born privileged.” I asked what that meant. Because from where I stand: • I built my life • I carry myself a certain way • I know what I bring into a room If we are in the same room, at the same table, who holds more privilege? We were invited for a reason. There are levels. There are ranks. But once you are in the room, you stand on your own. Know your place. Then stand in it. Sometimes you step forward. Sometimes you step back. Life moves like that. Do not confuse presence with permission. No one outranks your worth. Respect is felt. ⸻ Everyone, have a great Saturday. Take a day off. Look within. Ask yourself this. You do not see AI. No color. No gender. Yet you trust the response. Because the delivery shows up. Try this. Meet the next person you meet the same way. No assumptions. No labels. Start with presence. Start with respect. Peace.

I help growth-minded companies clarify who they are, what they stand for, and how they win in the markets they serve.

For more than two decades, I’ve worked in brand strategy, digital marketing, and public relations. My focus is on helping AI startups, technology firms, and competitive professional services organizations build positioning that translates into measurable traction. When messaging aligns with real capabilities and a clearly defined audience, marketing performance improves, lead quality strengthens, and teams operate with greater confidence.

My work typically includes:

• Strategic Brand Positioning – Defining a company’s distinct place in the market so every marketing decision reinforces authority and relevance.
• Go-to-Market Strategy – Connecting product, audience, messaging, and channel strategy into a cohesive launch or growth plan.
• Digital Authority & SEO – Building structured content ecosystems that increase visibility, support sales conversations, and drive inbound demand.
• AI-Powered Marketing Systems – Integrating automation and intelligent workflows that improve efficiency without sacrificing strategic oversight.
• Law Firm & Professional Services Growth – Developing authority-based marketing frameworks that strengthen reputation and client acquisition.

I’ve spent 30 years in both traditional and digital marketing, which gives me a practical view of how strategy translates into execution. Clear positioning shapes stronger campaigns. Strong campaigns create measurable outcomes. Measurable outcomes build sustainable growth.

Beyond strategy, I am deeply connected to the creative world. I have built thriving art communities such as the Alcohol Ink Art Society and Mixed Media Mad, co-founded CreateSmART Academy with more than 150 online courses, and co-authored two books on alcohol ink painting.

Whether I am supporting a startup through its first funding round or helping an established brand evolve its voice, my goal remains the same: to help great ideas find their audience and their edge.

Admit it. We all get by with a little help from generative AI. For those of you who use AI to assist with your writing, what are some of the prompts you use to remove some of the characteristics of AI-style content generation? Here are some of my go-to instructions... * Analyze my writing style in the text below for tone, word choice, and sentence structure. Use this style for all future outputs. ( I feed it something that I've written, of course.) * Please do not use words like: delve, robust, leverage, streamline, facilitate, tapestry, landscape, realm, unlock, unleash, meticulous, navigating, complexities, realm, game-changer, transformative, or pivotal." (I add to this list if it becomes obvious) * Please use informal language, include contractions (e.g., use 'don't' instead of 'do not'), and allow occasional sentence fragments or conversational fillers". * Do not use the rhetorical figure of antithesis or contrastive negation. Specifically, avoid any sentence that starts by negating a concept ('It’s not X...') only to reframe it ('It’s Y...')". * Please minimize the use of the word "just." * Please explain this as if you are a human telling a story, not a textbook. Include personal opinions, slightly messy thoughts, and active, non-linear phrases. * Please vary sentence rhythm. Make some sentences punchy and quick, while others are longer. * Please don't use emdashes. * Please don't place paragraph separators in output. * Please do not include emojis. 100% of the time, I end up rewording sentences because they don't sound like me (even though I've trained) It depends on what I'm writing... a blog post will look different from a cold email, obviously. And a press release has a totally different vibe. What are the go-to prompts that get you closer to finishing with AI-generated content?

About
I’ve spent 33 years and 120+ patents building technology. Now my work focuses on one challenge: giving people real control over their data.

My career began at IBM, where I helped shape the Linux strategy and earned a Chairman’s Award. At Intel, I served as CTO of the OpenFog Consortium and led IEEE working groups on distributed computing. Over three decades I’ve filed more than 120 patents and written 30+ technical papers that influenced how the industry thinks about edge computing, IoT, and distributed systems.

Somewhere along that journey, I realized we were building an extraordinary digital infrastructure on a broken foundation. People have no real control over data that defines their lives.

That realization led me to found Synovient.

The problem is simple to describe and hard to ignore. Your health data, behavior patterns, and digital identity are collected, analyzed, and monetized every day. Most of this happens without your awareness and almost never with meaningful consent. In healthcare alone, it has created an $84 billion market built on patient data that patients do not own, cannot see, and cannot govern. Now, as AI companies accelerate model training on human data, the extraction is increasing.

At Synovient, we are building true data sovereignty. Not policies, but architectures that embed ownership, consent, and control directly into data itself. Our partnership with MatchRite Care, which connects to 90 percent of U.S. health systems, is aimed at giving patients real ownership of their health data. We are also developing approaches that allow artists to protect their work in the streaming and NFT economy.

This is not a privacy or security problem. It is an architectural problem. We optimized for performance, scale, and efficiency, but never built for human agency. We now have a narrow window to correct that before AI systems become too entrenched to unwind.

I am building in public, sharing what we learn about data sovereignty, AI, and digital rights. I speak, write, and advise in these spaces because this is not just a business challenge. It is a structural question about the future of human autonomy.

If you are working on digital rights, data sovereignty, healthcare innovation, ethical AI, or technology that does not exploit users, I welcome the conversation.Show less

I’m currently building HousMthr, an AI-powered travel execution platform designed to coordinate logistics, safety, and shared experiences for groups traveling together.

Group travel is one of the fastest growing segments of the travel industry, yet the tools for coordinating shared trips — logistics, room assignments, expenses, activities, and communication — remain fragmented across multiple apps.

HousMthr is designed to solve this problem by creating a centralized platform for group travel coordination, enabling travelers staying together in vacation homes and destination communities to manage trips collaboratively in real time.

Before focusing on travel technology, my career has centered on building and scaling digital platforms and marketplaces across multiple industries.

I spent nearly a decade at AOL helping scale major advertising technology platforms including ADTECH, Advertising.com, and AOL Marketplace, one of the early programmatic advertising exchanges. During that time I led large enterprise teams and helped drive adoption across publishers, agencies, and global technology companies.

I later served as Chief Revenue Officer at NYIAX, a NASDAQ-backed marketplace designed to enable futures trading of advertising inventory. During the early commercialization phase I generated $18M in platform revenue while working closely with global publishers, agencies, and brands.

Across these roles my focus has consistently been on turning complex platforms into commercially viable markets.

This is a really interesting development in the AI agent space. A recent court ruling suggests that AI agents can’t act on your behalf on a platform unless that platform explicitly allows it. Even if you give the AI permission, the platform may still be able to block it. This raises a bigger question for the future of AI agents. A lot of the excitement right now is around agents that can log in, complete tasks, manage workflows, and essentially act like a digital assistant across the internet. But if platforms control whether those agents can interact with their systems, the whole ecosystem may end up being shaped by platform permissions and legal frameworks, not just innovation. Part of me understands the need for security and control. But Part of me also wonders if too many restrictions early on could slow down creativity and experimentation in this new AI era. We’re basically watching the rules of the agent economy get written in real time. Curious what others think: Should AI agents be free to act anywhere a user can? Or should platforms control how agents interact with their systems? https://lnkd.in/eK-WBXDz

Why Group Travel Is Still Broken Nearly 40% of leisure travel happens in groups — friends, families, reunions, events, seasonal house shares. Yet the tools people use to coordinate those trips are still fragmented. Vacation rentals have increased the complexity even further. The industry optimized booking — but ignored what happens after arrival. The Problem- Anyone who has planned a group trip knows the reality. One person books the house. Another handles groceries. Someone starts a group chat. Expenses get tracked in random apps or spreadsheets. Plans constantly change once everyone arrives. What should be an amazing shared experience often turns into logistical chaos. The problem isn’t travel. The problem is coordination infrastructure. When multiple people share a house, a schedule, and expenses, you’re essentially running a small operating system for the trip — but today it’s held together with texts, Venmo, and Google Docs. And the rise of vacation rentals and seasonal shares has made this even more complex. The Opportunity- Travel technology has spent decades perfecting the booking transaction. But once travelers arrive, the tools largely disappear. That creates a massive gap in the market. Instead of building better booking funnels, the next generation of travel platforms will focus on trip execution — managing what actually happens during the stay. Introducing HousMthr- That’s the idea behind HousMthr. HousMthr is designed as a Group Travel Execution Platform — built specifically for people sharing houses, trips, and experiences together. The platform helps groups manage: Room assignments Shared expenses Grocery and packing lists Trip calendars and events Real-time group communication Safety alerts and trip intelligence Rather than static itineraries, HousMthr acts as a co-pilot for group travel, helping trips adapt as plans change. The Bigger Vision- Travel isn’t just about where you go. It’s about who you experience it with. The infrastructure supporting that shared experience has barely evolved. 🧳 That’s the opportunity we’re building for. New Release this week!

I help founders move from guessing and worrying about their numbers to feeling calm, confident and in control of their finances.

Most startups don’t fail because the idea wasn’t good enough.
They fail because of cash flow issues, surprise tax bills, or not being investor-ready when the opportunity comes.

After 15+ years in finance, supporting founders across SaaS, tech and crypto, I’ve seen it firsthand:

• Revenue growing but cash still tight
• Reports that don’t tell the real story
• Investors asking for numbers the business doesn’t have
• Tax deadlines creeping up with no plan
• Systems that break as the business scales

I started Sagay Accounting to change that.

We work closely with founders so they understand their numbers, make confident decisions, and grow without financial stress.

What this looks like in practice:

• True cash flow clarity
• Investor-ready reports and modelling
• SEIS and EIS handled end-to-end (100 percent success rate)
• Real forecasting and tax planning
• No more financial shocks
• Calm and supportive finance partnership

We’ve helped founders raise and save over £2m, and today I am also building Tobi Assist - an AI finance assistant and bookkeeping partner that works directly through WhatsApp.

The goal is simple:
Real-time support, automated bookkeeping, and clear numbers for founders who don’t have time to chase spreadsheets or dashboards.

If you want clarity, confidence and a finance partner who grows with you, let’s talk.Show less

The new tax year starts in 7 days. Here is what is changing and what you need to do before 6th April. Change 1 — Dividend tax is going up: The basic rate on dividends rises from 8.75% to 10.75% from 6th April. If you run a limited company and pay yourself through dividends, that 2% increase matters. On £60,000 in dividends, you will pay £1,190 more in tax this year compared to last. Now is the right time to review how you pay yourself from your company. Change 2 — National Living Wage is rising: The National Living Wage goes up from £12.21 to £12.71 per hour on 6th April. If anyone on your team earns near that level, their pay rises automatically. If you have not already built this into your monthly costs, now is the time. For founders raising money this year: The SEIS allowance resets on 6th April. SEIS gives investors 50% tax relief on early stage investments, meaning a £100k investment only costs them £50k out of pocket. Every year I see the same pattern. Founders approaching investors in April and May who are still waiting on their Advance Assurance from HMRC. Advance Assurance is HMRC's confirmation that your company qualifies for the scheme. Investors need it before they commit. And HMRC takes weeks to process it. If you are planning to raise this year, apply now. Three things to do before 6th April: 1) Review how you pay yourself from your company. 2) Update your payroll for the new minimum wage. 3) Apply for SEIS Advance Assurance if you are raising. Your Q1 results are done. Your Q2 plan is what actually matters now. Here is what most founders get backwards. They spend hours pulling apart January, February and March. Revenue figures. Expense breakdowns. Why things were up. Why things were down. But they spend almost no time building a proper plan for April, May and June with the same level of detail. What already happened is fixed. What happens next is in your control. Before you close out March, sit down and build a proper Q2 plan. Not a rough idea in your head. An actual written plan with: Revenue targets broken down by month. What you expect to spend each month. When cash is coming in. Any big one-off costs you know are coming. Then ask yourself one question. Given what I now know from the first three months of the year, what do I need to do differently? The best founders I work with do not dwell in the past quarter. They take the lesson, update the plan and move forward. My April to June planning framework is dropping next week. But over time, it builds something revenue alone never could: ✅ Stability ✅ Clarity ✅ A business that can sustain real, lasting growth Discipline doesn't just support revenue. It attracts it. Because when your foundations are strong—your systems, your habits, your financial awareness—growth stops being a gamble and starts being inevitable. 💬 What's the one daily discipline in your business that you know would create the greatest long-term return if you did it consistently? About
Integral Ecologist | Conservation Career Coach | Founder and COO of the Saint Kateri Conservation Center | Consultant | Author | Conservation Planner I have over 35 years of experience in ecology, land conservation, native species and invasive species management, planning, and environmental consulting, as well as 12 years in business management. I retired from my day job in 2025 and continue to serve as a freelance conservation career coach and consulting ecologist. I have worked in the government, nonprofit, and business sectors, including with The Nature Conservancy, GEI Consultants, the Long Island Native Plant Initiative, and the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation. As a career coach with Integral Nature, I help students and professionals cultivate purpose-driven careers rooted in ecological principles, personal values, and faith. I help clarify goals, identify blocks, develop strategies, build confidence, address job burnout, develop resumes, and practice interviews.I founded the Saint Kateri Conservation Center 25 years ago to promote faith, integral ecology, biodiversity, and climate resilience. The Center's programs include Saint Kateri Habitats, Parish Arks, an Indigenous Peoples Program, and a Catholic land trust. I continue to serve as an advisor and COO for the Center, supporting its mission and programs. 🔹 Ecommerce Platform Management — We build and manage high-performing brands on Amazon, Walmart.com, and other DTC platforms. That includes PDP optimization, ad strategy, SEO, forecasting, and creative that converts.

🔹 Omnichannel Marketing + Product Development — From concept to shelf (or storefront), we develop and launch physical and digital products alongside fully integrated campaigns — from TV and print to shopper marketing and packaging.

About
The greatest challenge of artificial intelligence is not technological. It is making good decisions when everything accelerates.

For years, we were taught that the challenge was learning more technology. I’ve learned that this is not true. The real challenge emerges when technology moves faster than an organization’s ability to decide well.

Technology does not fail on its own. It fails when it is implemented without judgment, without context, and without accountability for its impact on the business and on people. That is where my work begins.
I work with leaders and organizations facing complex decisions in high-uncertainty environments—helping them answer the questions that matter before technology ends up deciding for them:
What should be automated?
What should not be touched?
Which risks are worth taking?
And which ones must be avoided?

My focus has never been on implementing tools because they are trendy.
My work is about helping leaders think before they execute—cutting through noise, setting priorities, and making decisions that are sustainable over the long term. I have seen how AI can accelerate chaos.
And I have also seen how, when designed with intention, it can restore focus, protect teams, and bring clarity back to those who carry decision-making responsibility.

That is why I approach transformation from a different place: by integrating business, technology, and the human factor—without hype and without shortcuts.

Through Lumaversa, the firm I founded, those strategic decisions are translated into real AI and automation systems that reduce operational friction and generate tangible impact.
Not as promises, but as conscious design of how work gets done. I work with leaders who understand that deciding well is a form of leadership.

Decisions that matter when everything around us accelerates.Show less

About
My career spans three decades, combining expertise in Lean/Six Sigma process optimization, organizational change management, and enterprise systems transformation. Initially in manufacturing, optimizing operations in industries like food, aerospace, naval military, and automotive. Later in management consulting, supporting global ERP implementations and driving transformational change. I’ve worked in Asia, Europe, and North and Central America. This experience has given me a unique ability to align complex systems with people-focused solutions.I am currently co-creating a bioregional initiative in southwestern British Columbia focused on building long-term food resilience, regenerative economic infrastructure, and place-based governance. This work brings together farmers, Indigenous knowledge holders, community organizations, researchers, and funders to map what is already working, surface systemic barriers, and enable coordinated action across the regional food system. We are in an early-stage prototyping phase, developing a shared systems map, grounded community narratives, and governance approaches that can guide aligned investment and collaboration over the coming decades. The goal is to create the conditions for a resilient, equitable, and regenerative food system that works for both human and non-human communities.I’ve been immersed in the entrepreneurial world the past five years, leveraging my corporate operational expertise and systems thinking to support transformational regenerative leaders in their work to restore living soil. Living soil is a critical lever for creating human, community, and planetary health. Over the past five years, I’ve deepened my understanding of regenerative agriculture, regenerative economy, decentralized governance, resilient communities, water stewardship, biodiversity, and human health, underpinned by the importance of systems thinking, Nature’s intelligence, and "warm data" for holistic success. My passion is to foster regenerative systems that leave everything better for everyone, shifting from profit-driven enterprises to syntropic models that build abundance and resilience. It has the potential to become a breakthrough to how we actually transform systems to create the world our hearts know is possible.I co-create with ThePivot.Earth team and our vast network of diverse experts to seamlessly weave together threads of regeneration, social cohesion, economic vitality, emotional resilience, mental fortitude, and spiritual elevation.Show less

About
From my small village in France to supporting expatriates in the United States

🌱 Where it all began

I come from a small village of 400 inhabitants near Saint-Étienne, where simplicity and solidarity were core values.
Fourteen years ago, I took the big leap and moved to the United States ; driven by ambition but faced with many challenges.
Discovering a new system, understanding its complex financial rules…
I made my own mistakes, lost time and money, especially when it came to retirement planning ; simply due to a lack of knowledge and guidance.

🛡️ Why I do what I do today

This experience naturally led me to specialize in protecting wealth and family, with a clear mission: help French expatriates avoid the pitfalls I encountered and work towards their long term financial goals with confidence.
Today, with several years of experience in financial services, I guide expatriates in making informed decisions tailored to their unique situation.

🌊 Beyond work

When I’m not working, I recharge on the water : kitesurfing, scuba diving, or sailing or by spending time with my two teenagers, who remind me every day why preparing for the future is essential.

🤝 Let’s stay in touch if you’re an expatriate, don’t hesitate to contact me. It will be a pleasure to connect with you.


Future communications may be in English only.

🌬️ The Wind of Change : Pourquoi le changement est votre meilleur allié (si vous êtes prêt) Le changement n'est pas un obstacle, c'est un moteur. Mais comme pour toute force de la nature, il ne suffit pas de le regarder passer : il faut savoir naviguer avec. Dans nos parcours professionnels et personnels, "The Wind of Change" souffle en permanence. Qu'il s'agisse d'une évolution de marché, d'une nouvelle technologie ou d'un virage stratégique, le changement est intrinsèquement bon car il apporte l'innovation et le renouveau. Pensez-y comme au kitesurf 🏄‍♂️ : Vous ne pouvez pas contrôler la direction ou la force du vent, mais vous pouvez contrôler votre aile et votre planche. Si vous restez passif, vous risquez de vous faire emporter. Si vous êtes bien préparé, vous pouvez transformer cette énergie en une glisse fluide et spectaculaire. 📈 La préparation, c'est votre aile et votre harnais : Anticiper plutôt que subir : Connaître le "bulletin météo" de votre industrie vous permet de gonfler votre aile avant la tempête. Saisir les opportunités : L'agilité, c'est votre planche. Un changement de trajectoire bien maîtrisé peut vous propulser vers de nouveaux sommets. Protéger ses acquis : Votre stratégie, c'est votre harnais. Il vous maintient en sécurité tout en vous donnant la liberté d'explorer. ⚓ Mon conseil : Ne craignez pas le vent qui tourne. Assurez-vous simplement que votre équipement est prêt, que vos lignes sont tendues et que votre harnais est solide. Le changement est une opportunité de croissance pour ceux qui ont pris le temps de s'y préparer. About
I founded AZTLAN Equity Management to help institutional investors gain exposure to unique opportunities in international small- and mid-cap equities through an investment approach that fuses art and science.

I come by the unique left brain/right brain balance naturally, having studied both art and chemical engineering in college. Following graduation, I worked as a chemist and dabbled in art during my free time.

My affinity for art also led to my move into the financial world: When I heard that a New York investment banker (and fellow Latin American) who offered me an internship was married to the owner of an art gallery, I said yes, secretly hoping the finance gig would open doors in the art world. To my surprise, however, investment banking evolved from an interest to a passion.

After a decade-long career as a banker, I took a job with an investment management firm and I spent about nine years working on a variety of roles in Emerging Markets funds, eventually becoming a director of equity research and portfolio manager. That was the ideal set up for launching AZTLAN Equity Management, where we uncover unique opportunities in international small- and mid-cap equity markets through the combination of quantitative analysis and fundamental research

To discuss our investment approach—or my latest foray into the art world—please contact me at 703-473-8020 or alejandro.garza@aztlanequities.com.Show less

Financial Data vs. Owner Instinct Many manufacturing businesses were built on owner instinct, which is based on: - Experience. - Relationships. - Deep operational knowledge. Those instincts are valuable. But as companies grow, financial data needs to start to play a bigger role in decision-making. Sometimes the numbers confirm intuition. Other times they challenge it. Which creates a tension many owners wrestle with. So here’s the question: When instinct and financial data disagree, which one should win? There are strong arguments on both sides. What do you think? I bet most business operators think that they know more than just some numbers tell them. I've seen that. But I know that as an accountant, you better look at the numbers.
Believe the numbers.

Profitability Isn’t Always From What You Think One pattern that surprises owners during financial reviews: The most profitable work isn’t always the work they expected. Sometimes it’s: • simpler jobs • repeat work • smaller customers • less complex production runs Meanwhile the “prestige” contracts often carry thin margins because they require more customization, engineering time, or schedule disruption. It’s a good reminder that revenue and profitability aren’t the same thing. So here’s the debate: Should manufacturers prioritize high-volume contracts… or protect their most profitable niche work? Both strategies have passionate defenders. What do you think?

Lily did another deal this time with AI. Eli Lilly and Company’s partnership with Insilico Medicine, up to $2.75B with $115M upfront, looks, at first glance, like a standard biotech licensing agreement. But when looking at this, if you’re Eli Lilly and Company, this isn’t just another deal, it looks like a hedge on the future of how drugs get discovered. Also, it doesn't appear that Lilly is stepping into a validated asset like many biotech deals, but instead coming in before validation and hoping to underwrite the foundation and engine from the start. Because if AI-driven platforms can consistently generate viable drug candidates, then the bottleneck in pharma R&D starts to move upstream. And if you’re Lilly, you don’t wait for that to be proven at scale so why not take a shot now. My thoughts on some of the deal terms: • The $115M upfront shows real skin in the game, not just optionality • The multi-billion structure creates exposure to a pipeline, not just a single asset • The exclusive rights which is actually pretty normal in these type of deals because of course why wouldn't you want it when it works? In my opinion, from a strategic perspective, this is less about any one program and more about securing access to a repeatable discovery capability before it becomes fully priced into the market. It’s also a portfolio move. Lilly already has one of the strongest pipelines in the industry led with a strong GLP-1 funnel. Deals like this aren’t about filling gaps, they’re about compounding an already winning position and protecting against the next wave of disruption and fueled by a very tangible AI investment. However, as we have seen across the broader AI cycle some have eroded as fast as they were erected so let's see where this one lands. #Pharma #Biotech #AI #DrugDiscovery #Strategy #CapitalAllocation

Same Players There's a constant wave of tech layoff news and now there's some data that shows exactly how systematic this has become on heels that Meta is having another of layoffs with a couple hundred people which is not including the larger 20% number that was floated for future cuts. A study from Zety looked at more than 1,000 tech companies, and what they found wasn’t a series of isolated events. • Nearly a third of companies didn’t just do layoffs, they did them more than once. • Almost half of all layoffs came from companies that had already cut before. • And most of those repeat layoffs happened within a year. Welcome to the tech layoff loop. And as you can imagine loops like this change how an organization behaves. After the first round, people absorb it, they rationalize it and sadly they usually get back to work. After the second round, something more unsettling happens. The question becomes heavier and more self-centric: am I next? At that point, I believe you’re no longer operating in a culture focused on building. You’re operating in a culture focused on preservation. And that’s where the real impact starts to show up. Research out of Harvard Business School points to the downstream effects with declining morale, lower productivity, more defects, less willingness to take risks. But you really don’t need a study to see it. You see it in the absence of the kind of energy that actually drives growth and lives in stagnation of survival. As finance leaders, we’re trained to think about layoffs as a lever. A way to protect margins, extend runway, reset the cost structure. And sometimes, that’s exactly what they are. But when layoffs happen repeatedly within a short window, it’s worth asking a harder question: Was the original decision incomplete or was the environment never fully understood? Because once you enter that loop, you may never get out. Finally, it's sad to see this happen since from a financial metric standpoint these companies are doing well but the chase for margins just squeeze humanity out of the numbers. #Leadership #CFO #Tech #Layoffs #BusinessStrategy #OrganizationalHealth

End of an era, and ode to my office chair. I bought my Humanscale Liberty Chair back in 2007. At the time, it felt like a massive purchase. I was at EY (technically Ernst & Young at the time), sitting in those pristine Herman Miller Aeron chairs, and couldn’t quite justify spending that much money. The Liberty felt like the right balance, practical, understated, and built to last. Eighteen years later… it did exactly that. As I ease back into work from surgery and spend more time working remotely, the reality is the chair has finally reached its limit. The gel pads aren’t holding up the way they used to. But walking away from it feels oddly wrong given how much history we have together. Ok people, this wasn’t just a chair, it was there for everything outside the office… and then eventually, everything when the pandemic hit. Let's do a greatest hits what this Liberty has been through: • Four moves, each one marking a new chapter of life • Late nights after EY, unwinding, learning, and figuring out what was next • Thousands of hours playing guitar, writing music, chasing something creative • Teaching my wife to play guitar so we could perform Daylight by Matt and Kim at our wedding • Designing everything for that wedding in Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator • Creating for friends and family, baby showers, weddings, kids birthday parties • Learn how to use a Roland Corporation U.S. Juno-G synthesizer while using various tutorial DVDs • Played and hardly beat more video games than I can count And then 2020 hit…the world shifted, and so did work. This same chair that used to be for nights and weekends became where real professional decisions happened. And then the Liberty was forced to play a different kind of tune: • Leading finance, accounting, and operations remotely • Supporting acquisitions, integrations, and transformations • Raising over $200M in capital • Personally mentoring many mentees which led to the formulation of Forge Optima It makes me reminisce pretty damn hard just writing this, but this chair saw the full evolution. From something that supported my life outside of work to something that became part of the work itself. I went from a mid 20s guy trying to figure things out, to building a career, family and a life with purpose. We don’t think much about objects like this but sometimes they sit center stage of your life while everything else evolves around them. And then one day, you realize they’ve been part of everything. I'm curious if anyone else has something like my chair in their lives? It'll be fun to share. Either way, thanks for reading this, time to let this one go. #Humanscale #Nostalgia #HermanMiller #HappyFriday #OfficeChair

A child born in America in 2026 won’t just receive a name… They may receive a portfolio. Starting July 2026, the new “baby bond” accounts (Trump Accounts) will give eligible children a $1,000 government-seeded investment account tied to the market. At first glance, it sounds simple. But let me tell you what I see… This isn’t just a savings program. This is a shift in economic philosophy. We are moving from: “Get help when you need it” to “Own something from day one.” And I respect that. Because wealth doesn’t begin when you earn your first dollar. It begins when your money starts working before you do. But here’s the part people aren’t talking about 👇 Every child may start with $1,000… But not every child will have: • The same level of contributions • The same financial guidance • The same tax strategy And over 18 years? That gap compounds. Now let’s talk tax—because that’s where this gets real. • The $1,000 seed? Not taxable • Contributions? Likely treated as gifts (not income) • Growth? Potentially tax-deferred or tax-free if used correctly • Withdrawals? Favorable if used for education, a home, or a business • Misuse it? Taxes + possible penalties 👉 Translation: This isn’t just about investing… it’s about how you exit And if structured right, this quietly becomes: • A wealth transfer tool • A tax-efficient compounding vehicle • A strategic advantage for those who understand the rules Now here’s the question I’m watching closely: Who manages this money? Because we’re not talking about small dollars. We’re talking about billions flowing into financial institutions. And where capital flows… influence follows. Always. So yes—I like the idea. But I also understand the reality: Two children can start with the same $1,000… One will have strategy. The other will have access. And those are not the same thing. My takeaway? This program has the potential to change lives. But only if we match access with education. Otherwise… We’re not just raising children anymore. We’re raising portfolios. And not everyone knows how to manage one. — Dyron Bush

Every quarter, when Carl Sagan and I would grab a beer and talk about the universe and the startups we were advising, he’d say he wasn’t worried about people being wrong, but rather the people being proud of being wrong. There’s a difference between ignorance and performative ignorance, and if I knew Carl Sagan (which I didn’t), and if we went out for drinks (which we also didn’t), I think he would have lamented how comfortable the #startup ecosystem can be with ignorance. We all start from a point of not knowing, but when it becomes a brand, we’ve reached a problem state. Speed shouldn’t be an excuse to skip understanding, and founders shouldn’t confuse confidence with competence. If you want to build something real and survive the parts of the journey where intuition stops working, curiosity is a requirement. I like Sagan’s quote for the Startup Advisor series because it speaks against celebrating the things that keep us from getting smarter. If you’re a #founder, don’t worry about making dumb mistakes, but to avoid drifting into the cosmic background, be sure not to glorify them.

David Bowie is the perfect pick for this week’s “Startup Advisor” post. He didn’t just reinvent himself; he reinvented reinvention. Bowie treated reinvention as something alive and elastic, and he was always one version away from becoming something stranger and better. Judging by my engagement level, these posts are largely for my own amusement, but if you’re reading (thank you), and if you’re in the startup ecosystem, you know that founders are doing a version of the same thing. There is less lightning bolt make-up and there're fewer costumes, but the meandering reinvention path is never straight (or tidy), and it’s definitely never boring. Startups move in zigzags--it’s why I say they’re fraught with a thousand (daily) manic-depressive states. You think you’re building one thing, and then the market hands you a mirror and says, “What’s next?” Bowie understood that instinctively. He was a once‑in‑a‑generation creative who invited uncertainty. And that’s why he fits so naturally into this “Startup Advisor” series. He may not have built companies, but he built worlds. And when founders are at their best, they do, too. I write about #startups, the #founder_experience, and building durable companies in an AI‑accelerated world.

About
After a fulfilling healthcare supply chain career, Erich Heneke is a semi-retired small business consultant, where he has built a reputable accounting firm over the past decade. Erich has worked with a variety of small businesses and focused on their accounting / payroll needs, including a concentration in not-for-profit organizations, where he has served as fractional CFO and volunteer board roles. After building his business in the Rochester, MN market, Erich has now established as a community resource in the Des Moines, IA market.

Additionally, Erich has worked with individuals on their personal finance journeys, authoring a personal finance course that has been viewed by thousands of people around the world. The linchpin to this course are strategies that Erich himself utilized to retire at age 46 and step into charitable and disaster relief work, while donating large portions of his salary to charities that align with his personal values.

Erich has taught a variety of undergraduate and master’s level business courses during the past 15+ years, including Forensic Accounting, Accounting Principles, Auditing, Business Math, Financial Management, Microsoft Excel, Leadership, Operations Management, and Budgetary Management at St. Mary’s University (Twin Cities), Upper Iowa University (Fayette, Iowa), Rochester Community and Technical College (Rochester, MN) and Wartburg College (Waverly, Iowa). Finally, Erich has taught courses on business soft skills, including conflict resolution, emotional intelligence, managing people, and working across multiple generations, as well as consulted with companies large and small on finance and accounting topics, including, but not limited to, business acquisitions.

Erich Heneke earned his Ph.D. from Capella University and Masters of Business Administration (MBA) at the University of Minnesota. He is a Certified Management Accountant (CMA), Certified Financial Manager (CFM), Certified Fraud Examiner (CFE), Certified Third-Party Risk Professional (CTPRP), and is a Fellow for the American College of Healthcare Executives (FACHE).Show less

2mo ago
Consistency brings results. This weekend I completed my very first half marathon — the Dubai Burj2Burj. It was a fantastic experience and one that perfectly reflects the spirit, ambition, and collective energy of Dubai. When I first arrived here in 2006, running along the pavement and the beach in Umm Suqeim earned me some very strange looks. Few could have imagined the transformation we’ve since witnessed — not just in facilities and infrastructure, but in the scale, mindset, and diversity of the population itself. I was a late replacement for this race, with just 48 hours’ notice. A quick check of my Garmin revealed something slightly alarming: I hadn’t run at all since 24 December, when I completed a steady 6km. I had also never run more than 20km in my life — and that was many years ago. But as in business, nothing ventured, nothing gained. With encouragement from several members of our boot camp community — particularly David Labouchere, Christian Hewitt, CISSP, Ahmed Hamid — I decided to give it a go. What could possibly go wrong? I’m very pleased to say I finished within sub two hours, a loose target I’d set myself. What struck me most, however, wasn’t the time — it was the consistency. Each of the 21 kilometres was run at almost exactly the same pace: 5:31+/- min/km. And that got me thinking. In business, we spend a lot of time searching for the breakthrough idea — the silver bullet that will take us to the next level. What we often underestimate is the power of steady, disciplined, repeatable performance. Progress doesn’t always come from heroic sprints. More often, it comes from showing up, maintaining rhythm, and executing well — kilometre after kilometre. Consistency, it turns out, really does bring results.
